title: BOT 105 - General Botany
description: Structure, function, reproduction, life cycles, and phylogenetic relationships of major plant groups. Relationships of plants to other organisms and to human activities.

title: ESM 105 - Natural Resource Conservation
description: Broad aspects; history of humanity in relation to land use; human populations in relation to resources; history of conservation movement; present day conservation problems.

title: GSP 101 - Geospatial Concepts
description: Overview: scale, coordinates, geodesy, direction, projections, surveying, global positioning systems (GPS), remote sensing, geographic information systems (GIS), cartography; historical context illustrating how maps depict spatial relationships, chart power, convey authority.

title: ESM 200 - Inscape and Landscape
description: Examines how people view the world, how these worldviews originate, and how they influence our perception of the world around us.

title: SCI 100 - Becoming a STEM Professional in the 21st Century
description: This course provides an introduction to the academic skills of a student in STEM, an introduction to the practical aspects of these disciplines, and their role in our multicultural society.

title: MATH 101 - College Algebra
description: Topics include algebraic equations and inequalities; polynomial, rational, algebraic, exponential, and logarithmic functions; compositions and inverses; geometric transformations and properties of functions; difference quotients.

title: MATH 101I - College Algebra with Integrated Support
description: Algebraic equations and inequalities; polynomial, rational, algebraic, exponential, and logarithmic functions; compositions and inverses; geometric transformations and properties of functions; difference quotients. Open to students in Math placement category III or IV.

title: MATH 1 - Support for College Algebra
description: Integrated support for development of quantitative reasoning in College Algebra (MATH 101I). No degree credit.

title: MATH 102 - Algebra and Elementary Functions
description: In-depth treatment of exponential, logarithmic, trigonometric, and polynomial functions.

title: STAT 109 - Introductory Biostatistics
description: Descriptive statistics, probability, random variables, discrete and continuous distributions, confidence intervals, contingency tests, regression and correlation, tests of hypothesis, analysis of variance. Emphasis: methods and applications used in the biological and natural resource sciences.

title: ESM 230 - Environmental Methods
description: Introduction to quantitative tools for environmental problem solving. Basic modeling skills in the context of topics related to environmental issues associated with air, water, land/earth, and energy.

title: ESM 303 - Applied Natural History and Ecology
description: Biotic communities of the north coast of California and the identification, ecology and life history of the organisms living there. Includes basic principles of ecology, field techniques for studying organisms in the wild, and methods of collecting and recording field data.

title: ESM 305 - Environmental Conflict Resolution
description: Introduction to conflict theory as applied in complex natural resource disputes. Skill development in planning culturally appropriate and inclusive public participation processes, meeting facilitation, and conflict mediation. Comparison of options for nonviolent conflict management.

title: ESM 309B - Environmental Communication
description: This course is intended for advanced students who want to learn the basic theories, strategies, and techniques used to communicate a body of scientific knowledge to the public in a comprehensible manner.

title: ESM 325 - Environmental Law and Regulation
description: Overview of laws, policy, and institutions used to regulate natural resource management and protect the environment. Legal principles; property rights; federal, state, and international environmental legislation; and regulatory authorities.

title: ESM 435 - Grant Proposal Writing
description: Fundamentals of grant proposal writing, from conception of the idea to writing a coherent and persuasive proposal. Combines critical thinking, communication and quantitative reasoning skills, and critical evaluation of proposals.

title: ESM 410W - Geospatial Capstone
description:
Bring your multidisciplinary education to bear on real-world issues. Work with clients to apply geospatial methods and technology to environmental and social challenges. Develop professional analytic, communication and presentation skills.
Cross-listed with: GSP 410W

title: ESM 411W - Energy and Climate Capstone
description: Bring your multidisciplinary education to bear on real-world problems. Work with client agencies to evaluate and address critical energy and climate challenges in our community and beyond.

title: ESM 453W - Environmental Education and Interpretation Capstone
description: Bring your multidisciplinary education to bear on real-world projects. Work with client agencies to develop interpretive exhibits, panels, and programming, and with local schools to develop science based curriculum.

title: ESM 455W - Ecological Restoration Capstone
description: Bring your multidisciplinary education to bear on real-world projects. Work with client agencies to apply the restoration process, devise methods and goals for restoration, develop management strategies for restored sites.

title: ESM 475W - Environmental Planning and Policy Capstone
description: Bring your multidisciplinary education to bear on real-world projects. Apply research skills in response to a community client’s request for assistance with an environmental planning or policy topic.

title: CHEM 107 - Fundamentals of Chemistry
description: Terminal course. Fundamental concepts and applications of general and inorganic chemistry.

title: GSP 270 - Geographic Information Science (GIS)
description: Introductory course in Geographic Information Science and spatial analysis involving collection, manipulation, display, and analysis of geographically referenced data. Raster and Vector data, overlays, buffer, proximity analysis and SQL queries.

title: SOIL 260 - Introduction to Soil Science
description: Soil’s physical, chemical, and biological properties. Implications for land management. Identify soil parent materials; use soil survey reports.

title: BOT 350 - Plant Taxonomy
description: Identify ferns, gymnosperms, and flowering plants. Recognize families and key plants in the local flora.

title: ESM 355 - Principles of Ecological Restoration
description: Scientific basis for reconstruction of degraded ecosystems. Focus on practices designed to improve ecological structure and function, and meeting societal needs for sustainable and functional ecosystems.

title: ESM 425 - Environmental Impact Assessment
description: Legislative/judicial history and current implementation of National Environmental Policy Act (NEPA) and California Environmental Quality Act (CEQA). Practice analyzing and preparing impact assessments for development projects.

title: FOR 315 - Forest Management
description: Managing forest-covered landscapes to meet a variety of objectives by applying economic, sociological, ecological, silvicultural, and operational principles.

title: FOR 431 - Forest Restoration
description: Forest restoration at multiple spatial scales from stand to landscape level. Goals for biological conservation, carbon sequestration, economic viability. Restoration techniques and case studies. Managing invasive plant species.

title: RRS 306 - Rangeland Management Principles
description: Analysis of rangeland biophysical communities; management for sustainable human and environmental values; use by wild and domestic animals; historical and legal changes in rangeland management.

title: RRS 430 - Rangeland Restoration and Improvement
description: Treatments, developments, and structures to improve rangeland ecosystems, services, and function. Ecological principles in ecosystem management and restoration.

title: WSHD 310 - Hydrology and Watershed Management
description: Hydrologic considerations of forest roads, stream crossings, road drainage. Management influences on hydrologic processes and aquatic habitat; protecting salmonid resources.

title: BOT 354 - Agrostology
description: Taxonomy, identification, and relationships of grasses of North America.

title: BOT 355 - Lichens and Bryophytes
description: Biology, ecology, natural history, and taxonomy of lichens, liverworts, hornworts, and mosses. Emphasis: epiphytic habitats. Weekend field trips.

title: BOT 358 - Biology of the Microfungi
description: Morphology, genetics, classification, ecology, and economic importance of yeasts and molds. Emphasis on isolation, culture, and lab techniques.

title: BOT 359 - Biology of the Ascomycetes and Basidiomycetes
description: Morphology, anatomy, classification, genetics, ecology, physiology, and economic importance of ascomycetes and basidiomycetes.

title: BOT 360 - Biology of the Fleshy Fungi
description: Systematics, ecology, toxicity, biological interactions, and culturing of mushrooms, polypores, chanterelles, boleti, and puffballs. Emphasis: Northern California fungi.

title: ENGL 311 - Creative Writing for Environmental Justice
description: Write, analyze, and critique texts that explore questions of environmental justice. Quality writing considered for publication in Toyon Multilingual Literary Journal.

title: ESM 301 - International Environmental Issues and Globalization
description:
Cross-disciplinary examination of economic development, world regions, population trends, resource exploitation, sustainability, impact of resource extraction in key world locations, and increasing global environmental connectivity, integration, and interdependence.
Cross-listed with: GEOG 301

title: ESM 420 - Ecosystem Analysis
description: Inventory and analysis methods for ecosystems based on systems ecology, sustainability science, and resilience theory. Focus on human impacts and management efforts in local landscapes.

title: ESM 430 - Natural Resource Management in Protected Areas
description: Principles/practices managing natural resources in wildland recreation areas. Fire, air, water quality; erosion; endangered species; exotic species control; hazardous features. Case studies.

title: ESM 460 - Environmental Planning for Public Lands and Rural Communities
description: Environmental planning processes applied by state and federal agencies and rural communities to manage for desired ecological, economic, and social outcomes on public lands and across rural landscapes. Key themes: collaborative processes, community involvement, stewardship.

title: ESM 462 - Coastal and Marine Planning
description: Approaches, policies, and politics related to planning and management in coastal and ocean areas. Consider ways to balance coastal and marine ecosystem conservation with a variety of human uses.

title: ESM 469 - Field Course in Environmental Science and Management
description: Gain hands-on, outdoor experience through field activities and tours. Topics and locations vary.

title: FISH 300 - Introduction to Fishery Biology
description: Identification, life histories, and ecology of important freshwater and marine fishes. Principles of fisheries management and its relationships with management of other resources.

title: FISH 310 - Ichthyology
description: Biology of fishes and fishlike vertebrates. Anatomy/concepts of systematics of fishes; classifying fishes, particularly commercial, game, and forage species.

title: FISH 320 - Limnology
description: Lake formation and aging. Physical, chemical, and behavioral relationships between organisms and their environments.

title: FISH 476 - Ecology of Running Waters
description:
Characterization of the physical and chemical environment, adaptations, distribution, and interactions of riverine biota, ecosystem structure and dynamics, and response to human alternation.
Co-listed with: FISH 576. (Only one of the co-listed courses may be taken for credit. See Terminology for more information.)

title: FIRE 321 - Fire Ecology
description: Fire as an ecosystem and physical process. Fire history, fire effects, fire regimes; interactions with abiotic and biotic ecosystem components; managing fire in California bioregions.

title: FOR 374 - Wilderness Area Management
description: Paradox of “managing” wilderness; scientific, legislative, philosophical frameworks; managing human use of, and influences on, wilderness. Weekend field trips.

title: FOR 430 - Forest Ecosystems
description:
Environmental factors on tree, stand, and landscape dynamics. Investigation at physiological, population, community, ecosystem, and landscape scales. Analysis of ecological data, scientific writing, and presentation. Extensive field trips in region.
Cross-listed with: FOR 530. (Only one of the co-listed courses may be taken for credit. See Terminology for more information.)

title: GEOL 306 - General Geomorphology
description: Origin and development of landforms, landform classification, geomorphic processes. Methods of geomorphological analysis, topographic map interpretation, and aerial photo interpretation. May require two weekend field trips.

title: GSP 370 - Intermediate Geographic Information Science (GIS)
description: Data accuracy and quality, standard and advanced geospatial data models, data integration and analysis, constraint analysis, location-allocation analysis, metadata standards and documentation, geospatial ethics, industry applications of geospatial analysis.

title: NAS 304 - Native Tribes of California
description: Traditional cultures of native peoples: archeology, material culture, social organization, historical interrelationships.

title: NAS 309 - Traditional Ecological Knowledge and Indigenous Natural Resource Management
description: Emphasis on ethical engagement of Traditional Ecological Knowledge; best practices for Indigenous natural resource management and the critical importance of Indigenous sciences and knowledges to addressing and developing natural resource management plans and projects. Comparison of the cultural traditions and beliefs of indigenous people, with those of western science based management.

title: NAS 332 - Environmental Justice
description: Issues/concerns that led to Executive Order 12898 (environmental policies and conflicts between industries and those seeking environmental protection, including Alaska Native villages, “lower 48” tribes, grassroot community organizations).

title: NAS 366 - Tribal Water Rights
description: Federal/state water laws and Indian treaties; water problems on Western reservations as classic examples.

title: SOIL 360 - Origin and Classification of Soils
description: Factors of soil genesis; their interactions. Soil morphology/description; classification, emphasizing wildland soils.

title: SOIL 363 - Wetland Soils
description: The morphology, chemistry, hydrology, formation and function of mineral and organic soils in wet environments. Topics include identification, estuaries, peatlands, preservation, regulation and mitigation.

title: SOIL 460W - Wildland Soil Management and Erosion Control
description: Characterization, mapping, assessment, interpretation, and management of wildland soils; nutrient cycling, fire effects, erosion control.

title: SOIL 468 - Introduction to Agroforestry
description: Objectives and socioeconomic contexts. Multipurpose tree species; soil/tree/crop/livestock interactions; soil conservation; soil fertility effects.

title: WSHD 424 - Watershed Hydrology
description:
Hillslope and fluvial hydrology. Water quality. Watershed management: analysis, planning, cumulative effects.
Co-listed with: WSHD 524. (Only one of the co-listed courses may be taken for credit. See Terminology for more information.)

title: WSHD 558 - Advanced Climate Change and Land Use
description: Meets jointly with WSHD 458. Students enrolled in WSHD 558 are expected to carry out additional independent analyses of climate change and land use and deliver a lecture on an independent topic.

title: WLDF 365 - Ornithology I
description: Classification, life histories, ecology, behavior, and special adaptations of birds. Identification in field and lab.

title: WLDF 430 - Ecology and Management of Wetland Habitats for Wildlife
description: Historical, ecological, and management implications of manipulating wetland habitats to benefit wildlife.

title: WLDF 431 - Ecology and Management of Upland Habitats for Wildlife
description: Theoretical and applied considerations for managing upland habitats to benefit wildlife species.

title: WLDF 460 - Conservation Biology
description: Endangered species management, reserve design, conservation genetics, related concepts.

title: ZOOL 316 - Freshwater Aquatic Invertebrates
description: Identification, behavior, life history. Insects, crustaceans, mollusks.

title: ZOOL 354 - Herpetology
description: Biology, classification, anatomy, distribution, and life histories of amphibians and reptiles.

title: ZOOL 356 - Mammalogy
description: Comparative mammalian biology. Systematics, morphology, behavior, reproduction, physiology, ecology, zoogeography.

title: ZOOL 358 - General Entomology
description: Classification, identification, anatomy, physiology, ecology, behavior, control of insects.
